hub_subtitle: Business English para Finanças Corporativas, Entrevistas e Carreira Internacional

lesson.menu_title: What Should Have Happened -- Modal Perfects
lesson.menu_desc: Performance review + modal perfects (should/could/must have) -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 3 -- What Should Have Happened
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Felipe Pimenta | Aula 3 -- What Should Have Happened

listenings.text: Good morning. Let me open the quarterly review with the number, because there is no point hiding it. We closed eight percent below plan, a shortfall of two million dollars. Now, the root cause. For weeks everyone in this building assumed the problem was the sales team. It was not. The approvals cycle had stretched from seven days to twenty-one. That was the bottleneck. Two analysts flagged it in February, and here is the part I want us all to sit with: it should have been escalated to this leadership team in week two, and it was not. Somebody must have seen that number at the February close, because it was on the first page of the variance report. We could have caught it at the first milestone. From today there is one new rule, and it is not negotiable. Any variance above five percent reaches this room in the same week, not the same quarter. That is the mitigation. The turnaround starts now.

Thanks for making time. I want to give you my own retrospective on the quarter, from the team that owns the numbers. First, what my team could have done. We could have caught the variance at the first milestone, in February, instead of waiting for the month-end close in March. The signal was there. It was small, and we treated it as noise. Second, what I should have done. I saw the approvals cycle stretching, and I should have raised it with you directly rather than adding a line to a report. That delay is mine, not theirs. What I am not going to do is blame the sales team, because approvals were the real bottleneck and the data is very clear on that. Going forward, my team escalates anything above five percent the same week, and we review the cycle time at every milestone, not only at close.

items.situation: "Why did the problem never reach me?"
tips: It should have been escalated in week two, and it wasn't.
I own the accountability for what reaches this room.
Own it in one sentence — do not narrate.

items.situation: "What could your team have done differently?"
tips: They could have caught it at the first milestone.
could have = missed chance, no blame

items.situation: "Somebody must have seen this in February. Who?"
tips: The failure was the escalation, not the detection.
The hiring freeze, no — we couldn't have known.
The approvals cycle, yes — we should have acted in February.

items.situation: "Is the gap real, or just timing?"
tips: It might have been a timing issue — let me check the cut-off date.
Hedge when you are not sure. Never guess with confidence.
Any variance above five percent reaches you the same week.
Finish every review with the mitigation and the next milestone.
